candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: The restore branch recovered the anchor but still did not reclaim the accepted best, which makes one bounded PTX reopen the highest-value next test.
This family has near-best historical evidence and can be replayed with a single scheduler-facing change on the exact restored surface.
It now ranks ahead of another immediate restore because the current source already is the restore anchor.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 38 restored the exact accepted PTX surface and improved the runtime back to 24.17151993 ms, but it still finished about 0.007 ms behind the accepted best. That puts the loop back on the correct anchor and reopens the tight PTX exploit family that already showed the cleanest near-best evidence: retiming the initial wait/commit window on the same 128x128 PTX microkernel. Because the restore did not fully recover the best measurement, the next budget unit should be spent on this bounded reopen rather than immediately restoring again.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual cp.async wait-group timing and control-path latency in the PTX 128x128 hot-band microkernel after the exact restore surface has been re-established.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This family stays on the dominant PTX surface and historically built cleanly, but its upside is measured in hundredths of a millisecond and it is easy to replay a closed negative variant if the change grows beyond one bounded wait/commit-window retime.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus 24.17151993 ms and the accepted 24.16427231 ms anchor
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the reopen does not beat the restored anchor or if it broadens beyond a single prologue wait/commit-window retime.

candidates.direction_name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
ranking_notes: The restore family remains the deterministic fallback whenever the active PTX reopen fails.
It ranks second only because the current source already matches that restored surface.
candidates.hypothesis: If the bounded PTX reopen misses again, the exact restore should remain the deterministic fallback because it is still the cleanest plateau anchor in the live search state. Keeping it in the top three preserves the shortest path back to the accepted surface after any new micro-retime loss.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Search drift away from the accepted PTX anchor, not a missing structural branch.
candidates.risk: Low. This is the known-good restore surface.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if another restore replay no longer reproduces the expected plateau or if build/correctness diverges unexpectedly.

candidates.direction_name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
ranking_notes: Keep the structural pivot live because the PTX plateau still has limited upside.
Defer it one more round because the cheaper PTX reopen can still answer a near-anchor question without taking the full geometry risk.
candidates.hypothesis: The 128x128 PTX surface is still hovering around the same narrow plateau, so the 256x128 pivot remains the best structural branch to keep live behind the tighter PTX reopen. It stays ranked third because round 39 should first spend one more budget unit on the cheaper near-anchor PTX family before escalating back to a high-variance geometry pivot.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Structural latency hiding and control amortization on the hot band, not another micro-retime within the same PTX steady state.
candidates.risk: High. This branch materially changes the dominant geometry and has historically carried large regression risk.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot still lands in the large-regression regime or if it requires broader writer-ownership surgery than the bounded transplant plan allows.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
